---
title: "NHANES-MetRisk Build Log"
author: "Debasrija"
date: "2026-05-08"
output: html_document
---

# nhanes-metrisk Build Journal

A running account of how this project was built and why. 


## Why This Project Exists

The goal was to build a portfolio project for a health data analyst role, while also catering to my growing interest in LLMs and AI in general. Initially, my idea was a health data analytics pipeline with an AI layer for summarising statistics. However, from the experience of an MVP I made earlier, I realised that it is necessary to evaluate outputs properly. So I am looking to include more formal methods of evaluation instead of reactively fixing bugs while manually testing the application.    


## Technical Decisions

**DuckDB: **
The natural question is why bother with a database at all when the data fits comfortably in memory. First, the planned AI layer needs a SQL backend: R functions executing DuckDB queries and returning aggregated results, which requires a queryable store. Second, it becomes a pipeline with it, over just being a simple notebook or script. DuckDB has extensive documentation that makes it easy to use.

**OMOP CDM: **
OMOP is the standard that makes health data interoperable. This becomes a good opportunity for me to see how this schema plays out with a dataset.

**Gemma 4: **
Free tier on Google's Gemini API gives 1,500 requests/day with unlimited tokens for Gemma 4. So no cost ceiling while testing. 

**No NL to SQL: **
NL-to-SQL exposes the data to the LLM, and an injection style prompt could return individual-level patient rows to the model. The plan is to let R functions execute DuckDB aggregation queries and return JSON summaries. Nonetheless, there will be guardrails that will further prevent these prompts from returning anything.


## Iteration 1 - OMOP Conversion


The minimal end-to-end pipeline that converts NHANES to OMOP CDM. Demographics, blood pressure, cholesterol in five files, and six LOINC codes. I would like to have every layer of this system working first: download, then OMOP mapping, DuckDB, analysis and Quarto report. 

### Project Setup - mostly notes for self

`.gitignore` has to be created before the first commit, not after. If a file is committed before it appears in `.gitignore`, git tracks it permanently. For a project involving API keys and patient-adjacent data, privacy is of prime concern.

`renv` is initialised before any packages are installed. The reason: `renv::init()` sets up a project-local package library and `renv::snapshot()` locks the versions. The "Explicit" snapshot mode locks only packages that are explicitly installed.

The `.env` file is created as an empty template now, as it will contain API keys, it should never be pushed to the repo, so that is taken care of in `.gitignore`.


### Downloading with `01_download.R`

After a few hiccups trying to download the files (the other option was to use nhanesA but I decided to use the download via link method from https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2017/DataFiles , because it is overall a more versatile method), getting the wrong URL and downloading the error page HTML instead of the required xpt file, I finally managed to get it going using `download.files()`. Finally, a `walk()` command is used to check the file sizes, which matched what was seen on the website.


### Making the person table with `02_map_persona.R`

There are 15560 rows in the DEMO dataset, out of which 9693 are adults. Out of them, 728 adults (7.5%) are RIDSTATR=1 (interview only), so they have no BP or lab measurements available. They will appear as NAs in analysis dataset.

For what I would need for the subsequent operations, only a subset of the 29 columns are required. The columns I kept are SEQN (as an identifier), RIAGENDR (to convert to gender OMOP style), RIDAGEYR (age in years, and to calculate the approximate year of birth - more on this later), RIDRETH3 (race, converted to OMOP later with the race concept ID, race label and ethnicity), RIDSTATR (as discussed above), and WTMECPRP (survey weights). The year of birth is approximated as 2018 − RIDAGEYR, 2018 being the midpoint of the collection window and most samples in this set coming from the 2017-18 set as the next one was prematurely cut off by COVID. 

Additionally, Hispanic participants (RIDRETH3 1 and 2) were assigned ethnicity concept ID 38003563; race_concept_id also set to 38003563 as no dedicated race concept exists for Hispanic origin in OMOP. race_concept_id=0 was assigned to RIDRETH3=7 (Other/Multiracial) corresponding to the use of 0 in OMOP in the case of an unknown concept.


### Mapping each test to a standard LOINC code with `03_map_measurement.R`

LOINC is the international standard for identifying lab tests, clinical measurements and documents. So each of the tests here have a specific LOINC code. The actual list of codes is massive, so I decided to manually map each test to its code. 

For SBP and DBP, NHANES switched to oscillometric measurement from auscultatory 2017 onwards (which unfortunately makes it difficult to compare measurements before that). I went with the average of three measurements, so although the LOINC codes for the individual measurements are 8480-6 and 8462-4, the average is denoted by a different code which I used - 96608-5 for SBP and 96609-3 for DBP. I have not controlled for arm cuff size and arm used for measurement here, which is a deliberate simplification which should alter insights only minimally.

Although lipids are provided in both mg/dL and mmol/L, I used only mg/dL, and hence the appropriate LOINC codes in tow.

For triglycerides I considered the fasting-specific code 3048-6 since P_TRIGLY uses a fasting subsample weight. However, after re-checking the documentation I realised my first assumption about triglycerides being present for all participants was wrong and it was present only for the fasting subsample. Hence this consideration eventually was proved right.

Three pre-calculated LDL methods are available: Friedewald, Martin-Hopkins, and NIH Equation 2. I was inclined to use Martin-Hopkins for its improved accuracy in high-TG patients, which is relevant for a metabolic syndrome cohort. However, NHANES sets Martin-Hopkins to missing for TG over 400 mg/dL, which removes that advantage altogether. The accuracy advantage of Martin-Hopkins is primarily at low LDL values (below 70 mg/dL), well below the derived flags used in this project (160 or more, 190 or more mg/dL). Finally, I retained Friedelwald as it is well established in literature. 

This brings us to six final LOINC codes. The measurement_date is taken as 1st June 2018 as the approximate midpoint of the collection window to not keep it as NAs, as the real dates are suppressed to protect participant privacy. However, because of this, the date cannot be used as any useful information.
